---
title: "Day 3 In-class Assignment"
engine: julia
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Submission and grading

- This assignment is graded based on effort. Edit this QMD file and submit it within 24 hours to receive full credit.
- If you finish during class, you may leave, work on the weekly assignment, or do your own work.
- Please do not hesitate to ask the instructor any questions.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="How to Complete This Assignment"}
- This assignment focuses on Git operations, so all Julia code is provided.
- The Git commands are included as a reference. You may use AI tools or `lazygit` instead; you do not need to use these exact commands.
:::

## Question 1: Set up the Git environment on your computer

1. If you have not already done so, install Git and the GitHub CLI (`gh`), and create a GitHub account.
2. Configure Git with your name and the email address associated with your GitHub account.

    ```bash
    git config --global user.name "Your Name"
    git config --global user.email "your-email@example.com"
    git config --global --list
    ```

3. Log in to GitHub and verify the authentication.

    ```bash
    gh auth login
    gh auth status
    ```

## Question 2: Set up your repository for this class

1. On the GitHub website, create a repository named `julia-empirical-economics-assignment`.
    - Set its visibility to **Private** so that your coursework is not publicly accessible.
    - Initialize the repository with a README.
2. Add `kosukeshimamoto` as a collaborator under **Settings > Collaborators**.
3. Clone the repository and enter its directory. Replace `YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME` with your information.

    ```bash
    git clone https://github.com/YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME/julia-empirical-economics-assignment.git
    cd julia-empirical-economics-assignment
    ```

::: {.callout-note title="Optional: Clone with ghq"}
- If you already use ghq, you may use it instead of the preceding `git clone` command.

    ```bash
    ghq get YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME/julia-empirical-economics-assignment
    cd "$(ghq root)/github.com/YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME/julia-empirical-economics-assignment"
    ```
:::

## Question 3: Complete the initial repository setup

1. Create the following directory structure.

    ```text
    julia-empirical-economics-assignment/
    ├── inclass-assignment/
    │   ├── day1-assignment.qmd
    │   └── day2-assignment.qmd
    ├── weekly-assignment/
    ├── final-project/
    ├── src/
    ├── scripts/
    ├── Makefile
    └── README.md
    ```

::: {.callout-note title="Empty Directories"}
Git does not track empty directories. It is fine if an empty directory does not appear on GitHub until you add a file to it.
:::

2. Place the Day 1 and Day 2 in-class assignment files in `inclass-assignment/`.
3. Inspect, stage, and commit the initial repository structure.

    ```bash
    git status
    git diff
    git add .
    git diff --staged
    git commit -m "Set up coursework repository"
    ```

4. Push the commit and inspect the history.

    ```bash
    git push
    git log --oneline
    ```

5. On GitHub, confirm that the files and the new commit appear in the repository.

## Question 4: Get and start the Day 3 in-class assignment

1. If the [public course repository](https://github.com/kosukeshimamoto/julia-empirical-economics) is not on your computer, clone it into the directory that contains your private assignment repository.

    ```bash
    git clone https://github.com/kosukeshimamoto/julia-empirical-economics.git
    ```

2. If you cloned the public course repository earlier, open it in your terminal and pull the latest version.

    ```bash
    git pull
    ```

3. Open your private assignment repository in your terminal. Create and switch to a branch named `day3-assignment`.

    ```bash
    git switch -c day3-assignment
    git branch --show-current
    ```

4. Confirm that the final command prints `day3-assignment`.
5. Using Finder or another file manager, copy `assignments/inclass-assignment/day3-assignment.qmd` from the public course repository into `inclass-assignment/` in your private assignment repository.
6. Confirm that `inclass-assignment/day3-assignment.qmd` now exists in your private assignment repository.

## Question 5: Add a Small Julia Workflow

The code below is provided because the purpose of this exercise is to practice project organization and the Git workflow.

The data-generating process is the linear model

$$
y = X\beta + \varepsilon.
$$

The OLS estimator computes $\hat{\beta}$ from the generated data.

1. Create `src/ols.jl` with a data struct, a data-generating function, and an OLS estimator.

    ```julia
    using LinearAlgebra
    using Random

    struct OLSData
        β_true::Vector{Float64}
        X::Matrix{Float64}
        y::Vector{Float64}
    end

    function generate_data(n; seed = 1234)
        rng = MersenneTwister(seed)
        β_true = [1.0, 2.0]
        X = hcat(ones(n), randn(rng, n))
        y = X * β_true + randn(rng, n)
        return OLSData(β_true, X, y)
    end

    estimate_ols(data::OLSData) = data.X \ data.y
    ```

2. Create `scripts/run_ols.jl` with a `main()` wrapper.

    ```julia
    include(joinpath(@__DIR__, "..", "src", "ols.jl"))

    function main()
        data = generate_data(1_000)
        β_hat = estimate_ols(data)

        println("True coefficients: ", data.β_true)
        println("Estimated coefficients: ", β_hat)

        return β_hat
    end

    if abspath(PROGRAM_FILE) == @__FILE__
        main()
    end
    ```

3. Use the following cell in `inclass-assignment/day3-assignment.qmd` to load the script and call the wrapper.

    ```{julia}
    script_path = joinpath(@__DIR__, "..", "scripts", "run_ols.jl")

    if isfile(script_path)
        include(script_path)
        estimated_coefficients = main()
    else
        @info "Create scripts/run_ols.jl before running this cell."
    end
    ```

    The estimated coefficients should be close to the true coefficients `[1.0, 2.0]`.

4. Create the following `Makefile`. The command under `run:` must begin with a tab.

    ```makefile
    .PHONY: run

    run:
    	julia scripts/run_ols.jl
    ```

    From the repository root, run the script and verify that it prints the true and estimated coefficients.

    ```bash
    make run
    ```

5. Inspect, stage, and commit the completed work.

    ```bash
    git status
    git diff
    git add src/ols.jl scripts/run_ols.jl inclass-assignment/day3-assignment.qmd Makefile
    git diff --staged
    git commit -m "Complete Day 3 assignment"
    ```

## Question 6: Finish your work

1. Push the branch to GitHub.

    ```bash
    git push --set-upstream origin day3-assignment
    ```

2. Open a pull request.

    ```bash
    gh pr create \
        --title "Complete Day 3 assignment" \
        --body "I finished the Day 3 homework. Please review it."
    ```

3. Add the instructor as a reviewer.

    ```bash
    gh pr edit --add-reviewer kosukeshimamoto
    ```

- From now on, you will work on your code in this folder.
- You can submit your homework by creating a branch and opening a pull request.
- The commit time is treated as the submission time.
